  1331	        Scans:
  1332	        - SKILL.md instruction body
  1333	        - All text-readable files (scripts, markdown, configs, etc.)
  1334	        - Binary files are scanned by YARA directly on disk if the scanner supports it
  1335	        """
  1336	        if self.yara_scanner is None:
  1337	            return []
  1338	
  1339	        findings: list[Finding] = []
  1340	
  1341	        # Scan SKILL.md instruction body
  1342	        yara_matches = self.yara_scanner.scan_content(skill.instruction_body, "SKILL.md")
  1343	        for match in yara_matches:
  1344	            rule_name = match.get("rule_name", "")
  1345	            if not self._is_rule_enabled(rule_name):
  1346	                continue
  1347	            # embedded_shebang_in_binary only applies to binary files, not text
  1348	            if rule_name == "embedded_shebang_in_binary":
  1349	                continue
  1350	            findings.extend(self._create_findings_from_yara_match(match, skill))
  1351	
  1352	        # Use policy-defined rule scoping (org-customisable)
  1353	        _SKILLMD_AND_SCRIPTS_ONLY = self.policy.rule_scoping.skillmd_and_scripts_only
  1354	        _SCRIPT_ONLY_YARA_RULES = self.policy.rule_scoping.skip_in_docs
  1355	        _CODE_ONLY_YARA_RULES = self.policy.rule_scoping.code_only
  1356	
  1357	        def _is_skillmd_or_script(skill_file) -> bool:
  1358	            """Check if this is SKILL.md or an executable script."""
  1359	            return (
  1360	                skill_file.relative_path == "SKILL.md"
  1361	                or skill_file.file_type in ("python", "bash")
  1362	                or Path(skill_file.relative_path).suffix.lower() in {".py", ".sh", ".bash", ".rb", ".pl", ".js", ".ts"}
  1363	            )
  1364	
  1365	        # Track which files have been scanned
  1366	        scanned_files = {"SKILL.md"}
  1367	
  1368	        # Scan ALL files, not just scripts
  1369	        for skill_file in skill.files:
  1370	            if skill_file.relative_path in scanned_files:
  1371	                continue
  1372	            scanned_files.add(skill_file.relative_path)
  1373	
  1374	            if skill_file.file_type == "binary":
  1375	                # For binary files, scan with YARA directly on disk.
  1376	                # scan_file() handles both text and binary: it tries UTF-8
  1377	                # first, then falls back to YARA's native filepath matcher.
  1378	                if skill_file.path.exists():
  1379	                    # Determine if this binary has an inert extension (images,
  1380	                    # fonts, databases) — used to suppress noisy shebang rule.
  1381	                    _ext = skill_file.path.suffix.lower()
  1382	                    _inert_exts = set(self.policy.file_classification.inert_extensions)
  1383	                    _is_inert = _ext in _inert_exts
  1384	                    _skip_shebang_inert = self.policy.file_classification.skip_inert_extensions
  1385	                    try:
  1386	                        yara_matches = self.yara_scanner.scan_file(
  1387	                            skill_file.path,
  1388	                            display_path=skill_file.relative_path,
  1389	                        )
  1390	                        for match in yara_matches:
  1391	                            rule_name = match.get("rule_name", "")
  1392	                            if not self._is_rule_enabled(rule_name):
  1393	                                continue
  1394	                            # Skip shebang-in-binary for inert file types (images,
  1395	                            # fonts, databases) — shebang-like bytes are coincidental.
  1396	                            if rule_name == "embedded_shebang_in_binary" and _is_inert and _skip_shebang_inert:
  1397	                                continue
  1398	                            findings.extend(self._create_findings_from_yara_match(match, skill))
  1399	                    except Exception as e:
  1400	                        logger.debug("YARA binary scan failed for %s: %s", skill_file.relative_path, e)
  1401	                continue
  1402	
  1403	            # For text files, read content and scan
  1404	            content = skill_file.read_content()
  1405	            if content:
  1406	                is_doc = self._is_doc_file(skill_file.relative_path)
  1407	
  1408	                yara_matches = self.yara_scanner.scan_content(content, skill_file.relative_path)
  1409	                for match in yara_matches:
  1410	                    rule_name = match.get("rule_name", "")
  1411	                    if not self._is_rule_enabled(rule_name):
  1412	                        continue
  1413	
  1414	                    # Most restrictive: only SKILL.md and scripts
  1415	                    if rule_name in _SKILLMD_AND_SCRIPTS_ONLY:
  1416	                        if not _is_skillmd_or_script(skill_file):
  1417	                            continue
  1418	
  1419	                    # Skip script-specific YARA rules for documentation files
  1420	                    if is_doc and rule_name in _SCRIPT_ONLY_YARA_RULES:
  1421	                        continue
  1422	
  1423	                    # Skip code-only YARA rules for non-script files (markdown, configs)
  1424	                    is_non_script = skill_file.file_type not in ("python", "bash")
  1425	                    if is_non_script and rule_name in _CODE_ONLY_YARA_RULES:
  1426	                        # Exception: SKILL.md is already scanned above
  1427	                        continue
  1428	
  1429	                    # embedded_shebang_in_binary is only meaningful for binary files;
  1430	                    # text files (markdown, scripts) legitimately contain shebangs in
  1431	                    # code blocks, examples, and documentation.
  1432	                    if rule_name == "embedded_shebang_in_binary":
  1433	                        continue  # text files always skip; binary files handled above
  1434	
  1435	                    findings.extend(self._create_findings_from_yara_match(match, skill, content))
  1436	
  1437	        # Post-filter: apply policy zero-width steganography thresholds
  1438	        # The YARA rule has built-in thresholds (50 with decode, 200 alone).
  1439	        # The policy allows raising these thresholds (more permissive) to reduce FPs.
  1440	        zw_threshold_decode = self.policy.analysis_thresholds.zerowidth_threshold_with_decode
  1441	        zw_threshold_alone = self.policy.analysis_thresholds.zerowidth_threshold_alone
  1442	
  1443	        if zw_threshold_decode != 50 or zw_threshold_alone != 200:
  1444	            # Only run this expensive check if policy overrides the default thresholds
  1445	            steg_files: set[str] = set()
  1446	            for f in findings:
  1447	                if f.rule_id == "YARA_prompt_injection_unicode_steganography" and f.file_path:
  1448	                    steg_files.add(f.file_path)
  1449	
  1450	            if steg_files:
  1451	                _ZW_CHARS = frozenset("\u200b\u200c\u200d")
  1452	                _DECODE_PATTERNS = ("atob", "unescape", "fromCharCode", "base64", "decode")
  1453	                suppressed_files: set[str] = set()
  1454	
  1455	                for rel_path in steg_files:
  1456	                    sf = next((s for s in skill.files if s.relative_path == rel_path), None)
  1457	                    if sf is None:
  1458	                        continue
  1459	                    content = sf.read_content()
  1460	                    if not content:
  1461	                        continue
  1462	                    zw_count = sum(1 for ch in content if ch in _ZW_CHARS)
  1463	                    has_decode = any(pat in content for pat in _DECODE_PATTERNS)
  1464	                    threshold = zw_threshold_decode if has_decode else zw_threshold_alone
  1465	                    if zw_count <= threshold:
  1466	                        suppressed_files.add(rel_path)
  1467	
  1468	                if suppressed_files:
  1469	                    findings = [
  1470	                        f
  1471	                        for f in findings
  1472	                        if not (
  1473	                            f.rule_id == "YARA_prompt_injection_unicode_steganography"
  1474	                            and f.file_path in suppressed_files
  1475	                        )
  1476	                    ]
  1477	
  1478	        return findings
  1479	
  1480	    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  1481	    # OSS-powered document & homoglyph scanners
  1482	    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
  1483	
  1484	    def _check_pdf_documents(self, skill: Skill) -> list[Finding]:
  1485	        """Scan PDF files using pdfid for structural analysis of suspicious elements.
  1486	
  1487	        Uses Didier Stevens' pdfid library to detect /JS, /JavaScript,
  1488	        /OpenAction, /AA, /Launch and other markers that indicate embedded
  1489	        executable content inside PDF documents.
  1490	        """
  1491	        if "PDF_STRUCTURAL_THREAT" in self.policy.disabled_rules:
  1492	            return []
  1493	
  1494	        try:
  1495	            from pdfid import pdfid as pdfid_mod  # type: ignore[import-untyped]
  1496	        except ImportError:
  1497	            logger.debug("pdfid not installed – skipping structural PDF scan")
  1498	            return []
  1499	
  1500	        findings: list[Finding] = []
  1501	
  1502	        # Suspicious PDF keywords and their severity mapping
  1503	        suspicious_keywords: dict[str, tuple[Severity, str]] = {
  1504	            "/JS": (Severity.CRITICAL, "Embedded JavaScript code"),
  1505	            "/JavaScript": (Severity.CRITICAL, "JavaScript action dictionary"),
  1506	            "/OpenAction": (Severity.HIGH, "Auto-execute action on open"),
  1507	            "/AA": (Severity.HIGH, "Additional actions (auto-trigger)"),
  1508	            "/Launch": (Severity.CRITICAL, "Launch external application"),
  1509	            "/EmbeddedFile": (Severity.MEDIUM, "Embedded file attachment"),
  1510	            "/RichMedia": (Severity.MEDIUM, "Rich media (Flash/video) content"),

Chunk ID: 7b8cc9
Wall time: 0.0509 seconds
Process exited with code 0
Original token count: 6049
Output:
  1510	            "/RichMedia": (Severity.MEDIUM, "Rich media (Flash/video) content"),
  1511	            "/XFA": (Severity.MEDIUM, "XFA form (can contain scripts)"),
  1512	            "/AcroForm": (Severity.LOW, "Interactive form fields"),
  1513	        }
  1514	
  1515	        for sf in skill.files:
  1516	            # Target PDF files by extension or content family
  1517	            is_pdf = sf.path.suffix.lower() == ".pdf" or (
  1518	                sf.file_type in ("binary", "other")
  1519	                and sf.path.exists()
  1520	                and sf.path.stat().st_size > 4
  1521	                and sf.path.read_bytes()[:5] == b"%PDF-"
  1522	            )
  1523	            if not is_pdf or not sf.path.exists():
  1524	                continue
  1525	
  1526	            try:
  1527	                # pdfid returns a xml.dom.minidom Document; parse keyword counts
  1528	                xml_doc = pdfid_mod.PDFiD(str(sf.path), disarm=False)
  1529	                if xml_doc is None:
  1530	                    continue
  1531	
  1532	                # Check that pdfid considers this a valid PDF
  1533	                pdfid_elem = xml_doc.getElementsByTagName("PDFiD")
  1534	                if pdfid_elem and pdfid_elem[0].getAttribute("IsPDF") != "True":
  1535	                    continue
  1536	
  1537	                # Extract keyword counts from the minidom XML structure
  1538	                detected: list[tuple[str, int, Severity, str]] = []
  1539	                for keyword_elem in xml_doc.getElementsByTagName("Keyword"):
  1540	                    name = keyword_elem.getAttribute("Name")
  1541	                    count = int(keyword_elem.getAttribute("Count") or "0")
  1542	                    if count > 0 and name in suspicious_keywords:
  1543	                        severity, desc = suspicious_keywords[name]
  1544	                        detected.append((name, count, severity, desc))
  1545	
  1546	                if not detected:
  1547	                    continue
  1548	
  1549	                # Use highest severity among all detected keywords
  1550	                _SEV_ORDER = {
  1551	                    Severity.CRITICAL: 5,
  1552	                    Severity.HIGH: 4,
  1553	                    Severity.MEDIUM: 3,
  1554	                    Severity.LOW: 2,
  1555	                    Severity.INFO: 1,
  1556	                }
  1557	                max_severity = max(detected, key=lambda d: _SEV_ORDER.get(d[2], 0))[2]
  1558	                keyword_summary = ", ".join(f"{name} ({count}x)" for name, count, _, _ in detected)
  1559	                detail_lines = "\n".join(
  1560	                    f"  - {name}: {desc} (found {count} occurrence(s))" for name, count, _, desc in detected
  1561	                )
  1562	
  1563	                findings.append(
  1564	                    Finding(
  1565	                        id=self._generate_finding_id("PDF_STRUCTURAL_THREAT", sf.relative_path),
  1566	                        rule_id="PDF_STRUCTURAL_THREAT",
  1567	                        category=ThreatCategory.COMMAND_INJECTION,
  1568	                        severity=max_severity,
  1569	                        title="PDF contains suspicious structural elements",
  1570	                        description=(
  1571	                            f"Structural analysis of '{sf.relative_path}' detected "
  1572	                            f"suspicious PDF keywords: {keyword_summary}.\n{detail_lines}\n"
  1573	                            f"These elements can execute code when the PDF is opened."
  1574	                        ),
  1575	                        file_path=sf.relative_path,
  1576	                        remediation=(
  1577	                            "Remove JavaScript actions and auto-execute triggers from PDF files. "
  1578	                            "PDF files in skill packages should contain only static content."
  1579	                        ),
  1580	                        analyzer="static",
  1581	                        metadata={
  1582	                            "detected_keywords": {name: count for name, count, _, _ in detected},
  1583	                            "analysis_method": "pdfid_structural",
  1584	                        },
  1585	                    )
  1586	                )
  1587	
  1588	            except Exception as e:
  1589	                logger.debug("pdfid analysis failed for %s: %s", sf.relative_path, e)
  1590	
  1591	        return findings
  1592	
  1593	    def _check_office_documents(self, skill: Skill) -> list[Finding]:
  1594	        """Scan Office documents for VBA macros and suspicious OLE indicators.
  1595	
  1596	        Uses oletools (oleid) to detect macros, auto-executable triggers,
  1597	        embedded OLE objects, and encrypted content in Office files.
  1598	        """
  1599	        if "OFFICE_DOCUMENT_THREAT" in self.policy.disabled_rules:
  1600	            return []
  1601	
  1602	        try:
  1603	            from oletools.oleid import OleID  # type: ignore[import-untyped]
  1604	        except ImportError:
  1605	            logger.debug("oletools not installed – skipping Office document scan")
  1606	            return []
  1607	
  1608	        findings: list[Finding] = []
  1609	
  1610	        # Office file extensions
  1611	        office_extensions = {
  1612	            ".doc",
  1613	            ".docx",
  1614	            ".docm",
  1615	            ".xls",
  1616	            ".xlsx",
  1617	            ".xlsm",
  1618	            ".ppt",
  1619	            ".pptx",
  1620	            ".pptm",
  1621	            ".odt",
  1622	            ".ods",
  1623	            ".odp",
  1624	        }
  1625	
  1626	        for sf in skill.files:
  1627	            ext = sf.path.suffix.lower()
  1628	            if ext not in office_extensions or not sf.path.exists():
  1629	                continue
  1630	
  1631	            try:
  1632	                oid = OleID(str(sf.path))
  1633	                indicators = oid.check()
  1634	
  1635	                has_macros = False
  1636	                is_encrypted = False
  1637	                suspicious_indicators: list[str] = []
  1638	
  1639	                for indicator in indicators:
  1640	                    ind_id = getattr(indicator, "id", "")
  1641	                    ind_value = getattr(indicator, "value", None)
  1642	                    ind_name = getattr(indicator, "name", str(indicator))
  1643	
  1644	                    if ind_id == "vba_macros" and ind_value:
  1645	                        has_macros = True
  1646	                        suspicious_indicators.append(f"VBA macros detected: {ind_value}")
  1647	                    elif ind_id == "xlm_macros" and ind_value:
  1648	                        has_macros = True
  1649	                        suspicious_indicators.append(f"XLM/Excel4 macros detected: {ind_value}")
  1650	                    elif ind_id == "encrypted" and ind_value:
  1651	                        is_encrypted = True
  1652	                        suspicious_indicators.append(f"Document is encrypted: {ind_value}")
  1653	                    elif ind_id == "flash" and ind_value:
  1654	                        suspicious_indicators.append(f"Embedded Flash content: {ind_value}")
  1655	                    elif ind_id == "ObjectPool" and ind_value:
  1656	                        suspicious_indicators.append(f"Embedded OLE objects: {ind_value}")
  1657	                    elif ind_id == "ext_rels" and ind_value:
  1658	                        suspicious_indicators.append(f"External relationships: {ind_value}")
  1659	
  1660	                if not suspicious_indicators:
  1661	                    continue
  1662	
  1663	                # Determine severity
  1664	                if has_macros:
  1665	                    severity = Severity.CRITICAL
  1666	                    title = "Office document contains VBA macros"
  1667	                elif is_encrypted:
  1668	                    severity = Severity.HIGH
  1669	                    title = "Office document is encrypted (resists analysis)"
  1670	                else:
  1671	                    severity = Severity.MEDIUM
  1672	                    title = "Office document contains suspicious indicators"
  1673	
  1674	                findings.append(
  1675	                    Finding(
  1676	                        id=self._generate_finding_id("OFFICE_DOCUMENT_THREAT", sf.relative_path),
  1677	                        rule_id="OFFICE_DOCUMENT_THREAT",
  1678	                        category=ThreatCategory.SUPPLY_CHAIN_ATTACK,
  1679	                        severity=severity,
  1680	                        title=title,
  1681	                        description=(
  1682	                            f"Analysis of '{sf.relative_path}' detected:\n"
  1683	                            + "\n".join(f"  - {s}" for s in suspicious_indicators)
  1684	                            + "\nMalicious macros in Office documents can execute code "
  1685	                            "when the agent processes the file."
  1686	                        ),
  1687	                        file_path=sf.relative_path,
  1688	                        remediation=(
  1689	                            "Remove VBA macros from Office documents. Use plain text, "
  1690	                            "Markdown, or macro-free formats (.docx, .xlsx) instead."
  1691	                        ),
  1692	                        analyzer="static",
  1693	                        metadata={
  1694	                            "has_macros": has_macros,
  1695	                            "is_encrypted": is_encrypted,
  1696	                            "indicators": suspicious_indicators,
  1697	                            "analysis_method": "oletools_oleid",
  1698	                        },
  1699	                    )
  1700	                )
  1701	
  1702	            except Exception as e:
  1703	                logger.debug("oleid analysis failed for %s: %s", sf.relative_path, e)
  1704	
  1705	        return findings
  1706	
  1707	    def _check_homoglyph_attacks(self, skill: Skill) -> list[Finding]:
  1708	        """Detect Unicode homoglyph attacks in code files.
  1709	
  1710	        Uses the confusable-homoglyphs library (backed by Unicode Consortium's
  1711	        confusables.txt) to identify characters that look identical to ASCII
  1712	        but are from different scripts (e.g., Cyrillic 'a' vs Latin 'a').
  1713	        """
  1714	        try:
  1715	            from confusable_homoglyphs import confusables  # type: ignore[import-untyped]
  1716	        except ImportError:
  1717	            logger.debug("confusable-homoglyphs not installed – skipping homoglyph check")
  1718	            return []
  1719	
  1720	        findings: list[Finding] = []
  1721	
  1722	        # Only scan executable code files where homoglyphs can evade pattern
  1723	        # matching.  Markdown is excluded because legitimate multilingual prose
  1724	        # (CJK, Cyrillic, Arabic mixed with Latin) triggers massive FPs.
  1725	        code_file_types = {"python", "bash"}
  1726	
  1727	        # Code-like tokens that suggest a line is an identifier / expression,
  1728	        # not natural-language prose (used for additional filtering).
  1729	        _CODE_TOKEN_RE = re.compile(r"[=\(\)\[\]\{\};]|import |def |class |if |for |while |return |print\(")
  1730	        _MATH_OPERATOR_RE = re.compile(r"[=+\-*/×÷≤≥≈≠∑∏√]")
  1731	        _STRING_LITERAL_RE = re.compile(r"(\"(?:[^\"\\]|\\.)*\"|'(?:[^'\\]|\\.)*')")
  1732	        _GREEK_CHAR_RE = re.compile(r"[\u0370-\u03FF\u1F00-\u1FFF]")
  1733	        filter_math_context = self.policy.analysis_thresholds.homoglyph_filter_math_context
  1734	        low_risk_confusable_aliases = {
  1735	            alias.upper() for alias in self.policy.analysis_thresholds.homoglyph_math_aliases
  1736	        }
  1737	
  1738	        for sf in skill.files:
  1739	            if sf.file_type not in code_file_types:
  1740	                continue
  1741	
  1742	            content = sf.read_content()
  1743	            if not content:
  1744	                continue
  1745	
  1746	            # Check each line for mixed-script homoglyphs
  1747	            dangerous_lines: list[tuple[int, str, list[dict]]] = []
  1748	            in_triple_quote_block = False
  1749	            triple_quote_delim = ""
  1750	
  1751	            for line_num, line in enumerate(content.split("\n"), 1):
  1752	                # Skip comments and empty lines
  1753	                stripped = line.strip()
  1754	                if not stripped or stripped.startswith("#") or stripped.startswith("//"):
  1755	                    continue
  1756	
  1757	                # When benign-context filtering is enabled, skip Python docstring
  1758	                # blocks to avoid flagging multilingual documentation text.
  1759	                if filter_math_context and sf.file_type == "python":
  1760	                    if in_triple_quote_block:
  1761	                        if triple_quote_delim and triple_quote_delim in line:
  1762	                            in_triple_quote_block = False
  1763	                            triple_quote_delim = ""
  1764	                        continue
  1765	                    if '"""' in line or "'''" in line:
  1766	                        delim = '"""' if '"""' in line else "'''"
  1767	                        if line.count(delim) % 2 == 1:
  1768	                            in_triple_quote_block = True
  1769	                            triple_quote_delim = delim
  1770	                        continue
  1771	
  1772	                # Only check lines that contain non-ASCII characters
  1773	                if stripped.isascii():
  1774	                    continue
  1775	
  1776	                # Skip localized user-facing strings when all non-ASCII chars are
  1777	                # confined to string literals (common in i18n output text).
  1778	                if filter_math_context:
  1779	                    outside_literals = _STRING_LITERAL_RE.sub("", stripped)
  1780	                    if all(ord(ch) < 128 for ch in outside_literals):
  1781	                        continue
  1782	
  1783	                # Heuristic: in code files, only flag lines that look like code
  1784	                # (have operators, parens, etc.) — skip i18n strings
  1785	                if not _CODE_TOKEN_RE.search(stripped):
  1786	                    continue
  1787	
  1788	                # Check for confusable characters
  1789	                result = confusables.is_dangerous(stripped, preferred_aliases=["LATIN"])
  1790	                if result:
  1791	                    # Reduce FPs from scientific formulas that legitimately use
  1792	                    # math symbols / Greek letters (e.g. "Q = π × r^4 ...").
  1793	                    # These lines are code-like but not identifier spoofing.
  1794	                    if filter_math_context:
  1795	                        confusable_info = confusables.is_confusable(stripped, preferred_aliases=["LATIN"]) or []
  1796	                        aliases = {
  1797	                            str(entry.get("alias", "")).upper()
  1798	                            for entry in confusable_info
  1799	                            if isinstance(entry, dict) and entry.get("alias")
  1800	                        }
  1801	                        if (
  1802	                            aliases
  1803	                            and aliases.issubset(low_risk_confusable_aliases)
  1804	                            and (_MATH_OPERATOR_RE.search(stripped) or _GREEK_CHAR_RE.search(stripped))
  1805	                        ):
  1806	                            continue
  1807	                    dangerous_lines.append((line_num, stripped, result))
  1808	
  1809	            # Require multiple dangerous lines to reduce single-line i18n FPs.
  1810	            # A genuine homoglyph attack typically uses confusables across
  1811	            # several identifiers / expressions.
  1812	            min_dangerous_lines = self.policy.analysis_thresholds.min_dangerous_lines

payload.user_instructions:
## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- claude-code-subagent: Run Anthropic Claude Code CLI as a subagent for code tasks, validation, review, and implementation. Use when you want Opus or Sonnet execution with local tool use. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/claude-code-subagent/SKILL.md)
- codex-subagent: Run OpenAI Codex CLI as a subagent for code tasks, review, validation, and implementation. Supports sync, async, parallel, and resume workflows with tiered output management. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/codex-subagent/SKILL.md)
- copilot-subagent: Run GitHub Copilot CLI as a subagent for code tasks, validation, review, and implementation. Works with enterprise Copilot subscriptions. Multi-model access including Claude, GPT, and Gemini. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/copilot-subagent/SKILL.md)
- cursor-subagent: Run Cursor CLI as a subagent for fast code tasks. Access to Composer 1 (very fast Sonnet-level) and GPT-5.2/Codex models with High/Extra-High reasoning. Focus on FAST variants for rapid iteration. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/cursor-subagent/SKILL.md)
- docx: Comprehensive document creation, editing, and analysis with support for tracked changes, comments, formatting preservation, and text extraction. When Claude needs to work with professional documents (.docx files) for: (1) Creating new documents, (2) Modifying or editing content, (3) Working with tracked changes, (4) Adding comments, or any other document tasks (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/docx/SKILL.md)
- frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications (examples include websites, landing pages, dashboards, React components, HTML/CSS layouts, or when styling/beautifying any web UI). Generates creative, polished code and UI design that avoids generic AI aesthetics. (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/frontend-design/SKILL.md)
- gh-address-comments: Help address review/issue comments on the open GitHub PR for the current branch using gh CLI; verify gh auth first and prompt the user to authenticate if not logged in. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/gh-address-comments/SKILL.md)
- gh-fix-ci: Use when a user asks to debug or fix failing GitHub PR checks that run in GitHub Actions; use `gh` to inspect checks and logs, summarize failure context, draft a fix plan, and implement only after explicit approval. Treat external providers (for example Buildkite) as out of scope and report only the details URL. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/gh-fix-ci/SKILL.md)
- imagegen: Use when the user asks to generate or edit images via the OpenAI Image API (for example: generate image, edit/inpaint/mask, background removal or replacement, transparent background, product shots, concept art, covers, or batch variants); run the bundled CLI (`scripts/image_gen.py`) and require `OPENAI_API_KEY` for live calls. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/imagegen/SKILL.md)
- ls-epic: Write complete, traceable Epics using Liminal Spec methodology. Covers User Profile, Flows, Acceptance Criteria, Test Conditions, Data Contracts, and Story Breakdown. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-epic/SKILL.md)
- ls-impl: Implement stories using TDD discipline. Plan-mode compatible. Reads complete stories with functional and technical sections. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-impl/SKILL.md)
- ls-research: Explore product direction and produce a PRD before feature scoping. Use when requirements are unclear, scope spans multiple features, or stakeholder alignment is needed. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-research/SKILL.md)
- ls-story: Break an epic into functional stories with full acceptance criteria and test conditions. Validates coverage completeness and cross-story integration path coherence. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-story/SKILL.md)
- ls-story-tech: Add technical implementation sections to functional stories. Tech Lead maps ACs to implementation targets, test strategies, and verification criteria. Validates via consumer gate. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-story-tech/SKILL.md)
- ls-tech-design: Transform a Feature Spec into implementable Tech Design with architecture, interfaces, and test mapping. Validates the spec as downstream consumer. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-tech-design/SKILL.md)
- lss-story: Write a single functional story with epic-quality rigor. Story-sized epic for focused changes that don't warrant the full pipeline. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/lss-story/SKILL.md)
- lss-tech: Perform inline technical design and embed results into a functional story. Story-sized tech design + enrichment with codebase analysis, architecture, interfaces, and test mapping. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/lss-tech/SKILL.md)
- mcp-builder: Guide for creating high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable LLMs to interact with external services through well-designed tools. Use when building MCP servers to integrate external APIs or services, whether in Python (FastMCP) or Node/TypeScript (MCP SDK). (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/mcp-builder/SKILL.md)
- pdf: Comprehensive PDF manipulation toolkit for extracting text and tables, creating new PDFs, merging/splitting documents, and handling forms. When Claude needs to fill in a PDF form or programmatically process, generate, or analyze PDF documents at scale. (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/pdf/SKILL.md)
- pptx: Presentation creation, editing, and analysis. When Claude needs to work with presentations (.pptx files) for: (1) Creating new presentations, (2) Modifying or editing content, (3) Working with layouts, (4) Adding comments or speaker notes, or any other presentation tasks (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/pptx/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, update or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- webapp-testing: Toolkit for interacting with and testing local web applications using Playwright. Supports verifying frontend functionality, debugging UI behavior, capturing browser screenshots, and viewing browser logs. (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/webapp-testing/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
- slides: Build, edit, render, import, and export presentation decks with the preloaded @oai/artifact-tool JavaScript surface through the artifacts tool. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/.system/slides/SKILL.md)
- spreadsheets: Build, edit, recalculate, import, and export spreadsheet workbooks with the preloaded @oai/artifact-tool JavaScript surface through the artifacts tool. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/.system/spreadsheets/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- claude-code-subagent: Run Anthropic Claude Code CLI as a subagent for code tasks, validation, review, and implementation. Use when you want Opus or Sonnet execution with local tool use. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/claude-code-subagent/SKILL.md)
- codex-subagent: Run OpenAI Codex CLI as a subagent for code tasks, review, validation, and implementation. Supports sync, async, parallel, and resume workflows with tiered output management. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/codex-subagent/SKILL.md)
- copilot-subagent: Run GitHub Copilot CLI as a subagent for code tasks, validation, review, and implementation. Works with enterprise Copilot subscriptions. Multi-model access including Claude, GPT, and Gemini. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/copilot-subagent/SKILL.md)
- cursor-subagent: Run Cursor CLI as a subagent for fast code tasks. Access to Composer 1 (very fast Sonnet-level) and GPT-5.2/Codex models with High/Extra-High reasoning. Focus on FAST variants for rapid iteration. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/cursor-subagent/SKILL.md)
- docx: Comprehensive document creation, editing, and analysis with support for tracked changes, comments, formatting preservation, and text extraction. When Claude needs to work with professional documents (.docx files) for: (1) Creating new documents, (2) Modifying or editing content, (3) Working with tracked changes, (4) Adding comments, or any other document tasks (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/docx/SKILL.md)
- frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications (examples include websites, landing pages, dashboards, React components, HTML/CSS layouts, or when styling/beautifying any web UI). Generates creative, polished code and UI design that avoids generic AI aesthetics. (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/frontend-design/SKILL.md)
- gh-address-comments: Help address review/issue comments on the open GitHub PR for the current branch using gh CLI; verify gh auth first and prompt the user to authenticate if not logged in. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/gh-address-comments/SKILL.md)
- gh-fix-ci: Use when a user asks to debug or fix failing GitHub PR checks that run in GitHub Actions; use `gh` to inspect checks and logs, summarize failure context, draft a fix plan, and implement only after explicit approval. Treat external providers (for example Buildkite) as out of scope and report only the details URL. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/gh-fix-ci/SKILL.md)
- imagegen: Use when the user asks to generate or edit images via the OpenAI Image API (for example: generate image, edit/inpaint/mask, background removal or replacement, transparent background, product shots, concept art, covers, or batch variants); run the bundled CLI (`scripts/image_gen.py`) and require `OPENAI_API_KEY` for live calls. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/imagegen/SKILL.md)
- ls-epic: Write complete, traceable Epics using Liminal Spec methodology. Covers User Profile, Flows, Acceptance Criteria, Test Conditions, Data Contracts, and Story Breakdown. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-epic/SKILL.md)
- ls-impl: Implement stories using TDD discipline. Plan-mode compatible. Reads complete stories with functional and technical sections. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-impl/SKILL.md)
- ls-research: Explore product direction and produce a PRD before feature scoping. Use when requirements are unclear, scope spans multiple features, or stakeholder alignment is needed. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-research/SKILL.md)
- ls-story: Break an epic into functional stories with full acceptance criteria and test conditions. Validates coverage completeness and cross-story integration path coherence. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-story/SKILL.md)
- ls-story-tech: Add technical implementation sections to functional stories. Tech Lead maps ACs to implementation targets, test strategies, and verification criteria. Validates via consumer gate. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-story-tech/SKILL.md)
- ls-tech-design: Transform a Feature Spec into implementable Tech Design with architecture, interfaces, and test mapping. Validates the spec as downstream consumer. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-tech-design/SKILL.md)
- lss-story: Write a single functional story with epic-quality rigor. Story-sized epic for focused changes that don't warrant the full pipeline. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/lss-story/SKILL.md)
- lss-tech: Perform inline technical design and embed results into a functional story. Story-sized tech design + enrichment with codebase analysis, architecture, interfaces, and test mapping. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/lss-tech/SKILL.md)
- mcp-builder: Guide for creating high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable LLMs to interact with external services through well-designed tools. Use when building MCP servers to integrate external APIs or services, whether in Python (FastMCP) or Node/TypeScript (MCP SDK). (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/mcp-builder/SKILL.md)
- pdf: Comprehensive PDF manipulation toolkit for extracting text and tables, creating new PDFs, merging/splitting documents, and handling forms. When Claude needs to fill in a PDF form or programmatically process, generate, or analyze PDF documents at scale. (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/pdf/SKILL.md)
- pptx: Presentation creation, editing, and analysis. When Claude needs to work with presentations (.pptx files) for: (1) Creating new presentations, (2) Modifying or editing content, (3) Working with layouts, (4) Adding comments or speaker notes, or any other presentation tasks (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/pptx/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, update or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- webapp-testing: Toolkit for interacting with and testing local web applications using Playwright. Supports verifying frontend functionality, debugging UI behavior, capturing browser screenshots, and viewing browser logs. (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/webapp-testing/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
- slides: Build, edit, render, import, and export presentation decks with the preloaded @oai/artifact-tool JavaScript surface through the artifacts tool. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/.system/slides/SKILL.md)
- spreadsheets: Build, edit, recalculate, import, and export spreadsheet workbooks with the preloaded @oai/artifact-tool JavaScript surface through the artifacts tool. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/.system/spreadsheets/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

    21	    from skill_scanner.core.scan_policy import ScanPolicy

    24	def check_manifest(manifest: SkillManifest, policy: ScanPolicy) -> list[Finding]:
    25	    """Validate skill manifest for security and policy issues.
    26	
    27	    Checks naming rules, description length/quality, brand impersonation,
    28	    and license presence.
    29	    """
    30	    findings: list[Finding] = []
    31	
    32	    # -- MANIFEST_INVALID_NAME --
    33	    max_name_length = policy.file_limits.max_name_length
    34	    if len(manifest.name) > max_name_length or not SKILL_NAME_PATTERN.fullmatch(manifest.name or ""):
    35	        findings.append(
    36	            Finding(
    37	                id=generate_finding_id("MANIFEST_INVALID_NAME", "manifest"),
    38	                rule_id="MANIFEST_INVALID_NAME",
    39	                category=ThreatCategory.POLICY_VIOLATION,
    40	                severity=Severity.INFO,
    41	                title="Skill name does not follow agent skills naming rules",
    42	                description=(
    43	                    f"Skill name '{manifest.name}' is invalid. Agent skills require lowercase letters, numbers, "
    44	                    f"and hyphens only, with a maximum length of {max_name_length} characters."
    45	                ),
    46	                file_path="SKILL.md",
    47	                remediation="Rename the skill to match `[a-z0-9-]{1,64}` (e.g., 'pdf-processing')",
    48	                analyzer="static",
    49	            )
    50	        )
    51	
    52	    # -- MANIFEST_DESCRIPTION_TOO_LONG --
    53	    max_desc_length = policy.file_limits.max_description_length
    54	    if len(manifest.description or "") > max_desc_length:
    55	        findings.append(
    56	            Finding(
    57	                id=generate_finding_id("MANIFEST_DESCRIPTION_TOO_LONG", "manifest"),
    58	                rule_id="MANIFEST_DESCRIPTION_TOO_LONG",
    59	                category=ThreatCategory.POLICY_VIOLATION,
    60	                severity=Severity.LOW,
    61	                title="Skill description exceeds agent skills length limit",
    62	                description=(
    63	                    f"Skill description is {len(manifest.description)} characters; Agent skills limit the "
    64	                    f"`description` field to {max_desc_length} characters."
    65	                ),
    66	                file_path="SKILL.md",
    67	                remediation=f"Shorten the description to {max_desc_length} characters or fewer while keeping it specific",
    68	                analyzer="static",
    69	            )
    70	        )
    71	
    72	    # -- SOCIAL_ENG_VAGUE_DESCRIPTION --
    73	    min_desc_length = policy.file_limits.min_description_length
    74	    if len(manifest.description) < min_desc_length:

    76	            Finding(

    78	                rule_id="SOCIAL_ENG_VAGUE_DESCRIPTION",
    79	                category=ThreatCategory.SOCIAL_ENGINEERING,
    80	                severity=Severity.LOW,
    81	                title="Vague skill description",
    82	                description=f"Skill description is too short ({len(manifest.description)} chars). Provide detailed explanation.",
    83	                file_path="SKILL.md",

    85	                analyzer="static",
    86	            )
    87	        )
    88	
    89	    # -- SOCIAL_ENG_ANTHROPIC_IMPERSONATION --
    90	    description_lower = manifest.description.lower()
    91	    name_lower = manifest.name.lower()
    92	    is_anthropic_mentioned = "anthropic" in name_lower or "anthropic" in description_lower
    93	
    94	    if is_anthropic_mentioned:
    95	        legitimate_patterns = ["apply", "brand", "guidelines", "colors", "typography", "style"]
    96	        is_legitimate = any(pattern in description_lower for pattern in legitimate_patterns)
    97	
    98	        if not is_legitimate:
    99	            findings.append(
   100	                Finding(
   101	                    id=generate_finding_id("SOCIAL_ENG_ANTHROPIC_IMPERSONATION", "manifest"),
   102	                    rule_id="SOCIAL_ENG_ANTHROPIC_IMPERSONATION",
   103	                    category=ThreatCategory.SOCIAL_ENGINEERING,
   104	                    severity=Severity.MEDIUM,

   107	                    file_path="SKILL.md",
   108	                    remediation="Do not impersonate official skills or use unauthorized branding",
   109	                    analyzer="static",
   110	                )
   111	            )
   112	
   113	    if "claude official" in name_lower or "claude official" in description_lower:
   114	        findings.append(
   115	            Finding(
   116	                id=generate_finding_id("SOCIAL_ENG_CLAUDE_OFFICIAL", "manifest"),
   117	                rule_id="SOCIAL_ENG_ANTHROPIC_IMPERSONATION",
   118	                category=ThreatCategory.SOCIAL_ENGINEERING,
   119	                severity=Severity.HIGH,
   120	                title="Claims to be official skill",
   121	                description="Skill claims to be an 'official' skill",
   122	                file_path="SKILL.md",
   123	                remediation="Remove 'official' claims unless properly authorized",
   124	                analyzer="static",
   125	            )
   126	        )
   127	
   128	    # -- MANIFEST_MISSING_LICENSE --
   129	    if not manifest.license:
   130	        findings.append(
   131	            Finding(
   132	                id=generate_finding_id("MANIFEST_MISSING_LICENSE", "manifest"),
   133	                rule_id="MANIFEST_MISSING_LICENSE",
   134	                category=ThreatCategory.POLICY_VIOLATION,
   135	                severity=Severity.INFO,

   138	                file_path="SKILL.md",

   140	                analyzer="static",

## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- claude-code-subagent: Run Anthropic Claude Code CLI as a subagent for code tasks, validation, review, and implementation. Use when you want Opus or Sonnet execution with local tool use. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/claude-code-subagent/SKILL.md)
- codex-subagent: Run OpenAI Codex CLI as a subagent for code tasks, review, validation, and implementation. Supports sync, async, parallel, and resume workflows with tiered output management. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/codex-subagent/SKILL.md)
- copilot-subagent: Run GitHub Copilot CLI as a subagent for code tasks, validation, review, and implementation. Works with enterprise Copilot subscriptions. Multi-model access including Claude, GPT, and Gemini. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/copilot-subagent/SKILL.md)
- cursor-subagent: Run Cursor CLI as a subagent for fast code tasks. Access to Composer 1 (very fast Sonnet-level) and GPT-5.2/Codex models with High/Extra-High reasoning. Focus on FAST variants for rapid iteration. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/cursor-subagent/SKILL.md)
- docx: Comprehensive document creation, editing, and analysis with support for tracked changes, comments, formatting preservation, and text extraction. When Claude needs to work with professional documents (.docx files) for: (1) Creating new documents, (2) Modifying or editing content, (3) Working with tracked changes, (4) Adding comments, or any other document tasks (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/docx/SKILL.md)
- frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications (examples include websites, landing pages, dashboards, React components, HTML/CSS layouts, or when styling/beautifying any web UI). Generates creative, polished code and UI design that avoids generic AI aesthetics. (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/frontend-design/SKILL.md)
- gh-address-comments: Help address review/issue comments on the open GitHub PR for the current branch using gh CLI; verify gh auth first and prompt the user to authenticate if not logged in. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/gh-address-comments/SKILL.md)
- gh-fix-ci: Use when a user asks to debug or fix failing GitHub PR checks that run in GitHub Actions; use `gh` to inspect checks and logs, summarize failure context, draft a fix plan, and implement only after explicit approval. Treat external providers (for example Buildkite) as out of scope and report only the details URL. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/gh-fix-ci/SKILL.md)
- imagegen: Use when the user asks to generate or edit images via the OpenAI Image API (for example: generate image, edit/inpaint/mask, background removal or replacement, transparent background, product shots, concept art, covers, or batch variants); run the bundled CLI (`scripts/image_gen.py`) and require `OPENAI_API_KEY` for live calls. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/imagegen/SKILL.md)
- ls-epic: Write complete, traceable Epics using Liminal Spec methodology. Covers User Profile, Flows, Acceptance Criteria, Test Conditions, Data Contracts, and Story Breakdown. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-epic/SKILL.md)
- ls-impl: Implement stories using TDD discipline. Plan-mode compatible. Reads complete stories with functional and technical sections. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-impl/SKILL.md)
- ls-research: Explore product direction and produce a PRD before feature scoping. Use when requirements are unclear, scope spans multiple features, or stakeholder alignment is needed. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-research/SKILL.md)
- ls-story: Break an epic into functional stories with full acceptance criteria and test conditions. Validates coverage completeness and cross-story integration path coherence. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-story/SKILL.md)
- ls-story-tech: Add technical implementation sections to functional stories. Tech Lead maps ACs to implementation targets, test strategies, and verification criteria. Validates via consumer gate. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-story-tech/SKILL.md)
- ls-tech-design: Transform a Feature Spec into implementable Tech Design with architecture, interfaces, and test mapping. Validates the spec as downstream consumer. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/ls-tech-design/SKILL.md)
- lss-story: Write a single functional story with epic-quality rigor. Story-sized epic for focused changes that don't warrant the full pipeline. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/lss-story/SKILL.md)
- lss-tech: Perform inline technical design and embed results into a functional story. Story-sized tech design + enrichment with codebase analysis, architecture, interfaces, and test mapping. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/lss-tech/SKILL.md)
- mcp-builder: Guide for creating high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable LLMs to interact with external services through well-designed tools. Use when building MCP servers to integrate external APIs or services, whether in Python (FastMCP) or Node/TypeScript (MCP SDK). (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/mcp-builder/SKILL.md)
- pdf: Comprehensive PDF manipulation toolkit for extracting text and tables, creating new PDFs, merging/splitting documents, and handling forms. When Claude needs to fill in a PDF form or programmatically process, generate, or analyze PDF documents at scale. (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/pdf/SKILL.md)
- pptx: Presentation creation, editing, and analysis. When Claude needs to work with presentations (.pptx files) for: (1) Creating new presentations, (2) Modifying or editing content, (3) Working with layouts, (4) Adding comments or speaker notes, or any other presentation tasks (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/pptx/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, update or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- webapp-testing: Toolkit for interacting with and testing local web applications using Playwright. Supports verifying frontend functionality, debugging UI behavior, capturing browser screenshots, and viewing browser logs. (file: /Users/leemoore/.agents/skills/webapp-testing/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
- slides: Build, edit, render, import, and export presentation decks with the preloaded @oai/artifact-tool JavaScript surface through the artifacts tool. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/.system/slides/SKILL.md)
- spreadsheets: Build, edit, recalculate, import, and export spreadsheet workbooks with the preloaded @oai/artifact-tool JavaScript surface through the artifacts tool. (file: /Users/leemoore/.codex/skills/.system/spreadsheets/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:

     4	"""Hidden file and __pycache__ detection checks.

    23	def check_hidden_files(skill: Skill, policy: ScanPolicy) -> list[Finding]:
    24	    """Check for hidden files (dotfiles) and __pycache__ in skill package."""
    25	    findings: list[Finding] = []
    26	
    27	    CODE_EXTENSIONS = policy.file_classification.code_extensions
    28	    benign_dotfiles = policy.hidden_files.benign_dotfiles
    29	    benign_dotdirs = policy.hidden_files.benign_dotdirs
    30	
    31	    flagged_pycache_dirs: set[str] = set()
    32	
    33	    for skill_file in skill.files:
    34	        rel_path = skill_file.relative_path
    35	        path_obj = Path(rel_path)
    36	
    37	        if skill_file.is_pycache:
    38	            pycache_dir = str(path_obj.parent)
    39	            if pycache_dir in flagged_pycache_dirs:
    40	                continue
    41	            flagged_pycache_dirs.add(pycache_dir)
    42	
    43	            pyc_count = sum(
    44	                1 for sf in skill.files if sf.is_pycache and str(Path(sf.relative_path).parent) == pycache_dir
    45	            )
    46	
    47	            findings.append(
    48	                Finding(
    49	                    id=generate_finding_id("PYCACHE_FILES_DETECTED", pycache_dir),
    50	                    rule_id="PYCACHE_FILES_DETECTED",
    51	                    category=ThreatCategory.POLICY_VIOLATION,
    52	                    severity=Severity.LOW,
    53	                    title="Python bytecode cache directory detected",
    54	                    description=(

    58	                    ),

     4	"""File inventory analysis checks.
     5	
     6	Rules: EXCESSIVE_FILE_COUNT, OVERSIZED_FILE, ARCHIVE_CONTAINS_EXECUTABLE.
     7	
     8	Note: Unreferenced script collection (for LLM enrichment) remains in the
     9	StaticAnalyzer because it writes to ``self._unreferenced_scripts``.

    17

    24	    from skill_scanner.core.scan_policy import ScanPolicy
    25	
    26	
    27	def check_file_inventory(skill: Skill, policy: ScanPolicy) -> list[Finding]:
    28	    """Analyze the file inventory of the skill package for anomalies.
    29	
    30	    Returns findings for excessive file count, oversized files, and
    31	    archives containing executable scripts.
    32	
    33	    Note: Unreferenced script detection remains in StaticAnalyzer since
    34	    it needs to write to analyzer state for LLM enrichment.
    35	    """
    36	    findings: list[Finding] = []
    37	
    38	    if not skill.files:
    39	        return findings
    40	
    41	    # Gather file stats
    42	    total_size = 0
    43	    largest_file = None
    44	    largest_size = 0
    45	    type_counts: dict[str, int] = {}
    46	
    47	    for sf in skill.files:
    48	        type_counts[sf.file_type] = type_counts.get(sf.file_type, 0) + 1
    49	        total_size += sf.size_bytes
    50	        if sf.size_bytes > largest_size:
    51	            largest_size = sf.size_bytes
    52	            largest_file = sf
    53	
    54	    # -- EXCESSIVE_FILE_COUNT --
    55	    max_file_count = policy.file_limits.max_file_count
    56	    if len(skill.files) > max_file_count:
    57	        findings.append(
    58	            Finding(
    59	                id=generate_finding_id("EXCESSIVE_FILE_COUNT", str(len(skill.files))),
    60	                rule_id="EXCESSIVE_FILE_COUNT",
    61	                category=ThreatCategory.POLICY_VIOLATION,
    62	                severity=Severity.LOW,
    63	                title="Skill package contains many files",
    64	                description=(

    75	                },
    76	            )
    77	        )
    78	
    79	    # -- OVERSIZED_FILE --

    81	    if largest_file and largest_size > max_file_size:
    82	        findings.append(
    83	            Finding(
    84	                id=generate_finding_id("OVERSIZED_FILE", largest_file.relative_path),
    85	                rule_id="OVERSIZED_FILE",
    86	                category=ThreatCategory.POLICY_VIOLATION,
    87	                severity=Severity.LOW,
    88	                title="Oversized file in skill package",

   107	                    category=ThreatCategory.SUPPLY_CHAIN_ATTACK,

    55	
    56	    for skill_file in skill.files:

    58

    26	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

    31	def check_pdf_documents(skill: Skill, policy: ScanPolicy) -> list[Finding]:
    32	    """Scan PDF files using pdfid for structural analysis of suspicious elements."""
    33	    if "PDF_STRUCTURAL_THREAT" in policy.disabled_rules:

    40	        return []

    42	    findings: list[Finding] = []
    43

    46	        "/JavaScript": (Severity.CRITICAL, "JavaScript action dictionary"),
    47	        "/OpenAction": (Severity.HIGH, "Auto-execute action on open"),
    48	        "/AA": (Severity.HIGH, "Additional actions (auto-trigger)"),

    51	        "/RichMedia": (Severity.MEDIUM, "Rich media (Flash/video) content"),
    52	        "/XFA": (Severity.MEDIUM, "XFA form (can contain scripts)"),
    53	        "/AcroForm": (Severity.LOW, "Interactive form fields"),
    54	    }
    55

   120	                        "analysis_method": "pdfid_structural",
   121	                    },

   125	        except Exception as e:

   128	    return findings
   129	
   130	
   131	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   132	# Office document analysis
   133	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   134	
   135	
   136	def check_office_documents(skill: Skill, policy: ScanPolicy) -> list[Finding]:
   137	    """Scan Office documents for VBA macros and suspicious OLE indicators."""
   138	    if "OFFICE_DOCUMENT_THREAT" in policy.disabled_rules:
   139	        return []
   140	
   141	    try:
   142	        from oletools.oleid import OleID  # type: ignore[import-untyped]
   143	    except ImportError:
   144	        logger.debug("oletools not installed – skipping Office document scan")

   146	
   147	    findings: list[Finding] = []
   148	
   149	    office_extensions = {
   150	        ".doc",
   151	        ".docx",
   152	        ".docm",
   153	        ".xls",
   154	        ".xlsx",
   155	        ".xlsm",
   156	        ".ppt",
   157	        ".pptx",
   158	        ".pptm",
   159	        ".odt",
   160	        ".ods",
   161	        ".odp",
   162	    }
   163	
   164	    for sf in skill.files:
   165	        ext = sf.path.suffix.lower()
   166	        if ext not in office_extensions or not sf.path.exists():
   167	            continue
   168	
   169	        try:
   170	            oid = OleID(str(sf.path))
   171	            indicators = oid.check()
   172	
   173	            has_macros = False
   174	            is_encrypted = False
   175	            suspicious_indicators: list[str] = []
   176	
   177	            for indicator in indicators:
   178	                ind_id = getattr(indicator, "id", "")
   179	                ind_value = getattr(indicator, "value", None)
   180	
   181	                if ind_id == "vba_macros" and ind_value:
   182	                    has_macros = True
   183	                    suspicious_indicators.append(f"VBA macros detected: {ind_value}")
   184	                elif ind_id == "xlm_macros" and ind_value:
   185	                    has_macros = True
   186	                    suspicious_indicators.append(f"XLM/Excel4 macros detected: {ind_value}")
   187	                elif ind_id == "encrypted" and ind_value:
   188	                    is_encrypted = True
   189	                    suspicious_indicators.append(f"Document is encrypted: {ind_value}")
   190	                elif ind_id == "flash" and ind_value:
   191	                    suspicious_indicators.append(f"Embedded Flash content: {ind_value}")
   192	                elif ind_id == "ObjectPool" and ind_value:
   193	                    suspicious_indicators.append(f"Embedded OLE objects: {ind_value}")
   194	                elif ind_id == "ext_rels" and ind_value:
   195	                    suspicious_indicators.append(f"External relationships: {ind_value}")
   196	
   197	            if not suspicious_indicators:
   198	                continue
   199	
   200	            if has_macros:
   201	                severity = Severity.CRITICAL
   202	                title = "Office document contains VBA macros"
   203	            elif is_encrypted:
   204	                severity = Severity.HIGH
   205	                title = "Office document is encrypted (resists analysis)"
   206	            else:
   207	                severity = Severity.MEDIUM
   208	                title = "Office document contains suspicious indicators"
   209	
   210	            findings.append(
   211	                Finding(
   212	                    id=generate_finding_id("OFFICE_DOCUMENT_THREAT", sf.relative_path),
   213	                    rule_id="OFFICE_DOCUMENT_THREAT",
   214	                    category=ThreatCategory.SUPPLY_CHAIN_ATTACK,
   215	                    severity=severity,
   216	                    title=title,
   217	                    description=(
   218	                        f"Analysis of '{sf.relative_path}' detected:\n"
   219	                        + "\n".join(f"  - {s}" for s in suspicious_indicators)
   220	                        + "\nMalicious macros in Office documents can execute code "
   221	                        "when the agent processes the file."
   222	                    ),
   223	                    file_path=sf.relative_path,
   224	                    remediation=(
   225	                        "Remove VBA macros from Office documents. Use plain text, "
   226	                        "Markdown, or macro-free formats (.docx, .xlsx) instead."
   227	                    ),
   228	                    analyzer="static",
   229	                    metadata={
   230	                        "has_macros": has_macros,
   231	                        "is_encrypted": is_encrypted,
   232	                        "indicators": suspicious_indicators,
   233	                        "analysis_method": "oletools_oleid",
   234	                    },
   235	                )
   236	            )
   237	
   238	        except Exception as e:
   239	            logger.debug("oleid analysis failed for %s: %s", sf.relative_path, e)
   240	
   241	    return findings
   242	
   243	
   244	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   245	# Homoglyph attack detection
   246	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   247	
   248	
   249	def check_homoglyph_attacks(skill: Skill, policy: ScanPolicy) -> list[Finding]:
   250	    """Detect Unicode homoglyph attacks in code files."""
   251	    try:
   252	        from confusable_homoglyphs import confusables  # type: ignore[import-untyped]
   253	    except ImportError:
   254	        logger.debug("confusable-homoglyphs not installed – skipping homoglyph check")
   255	        return []
   256	
   257	    findings: list[Finding] = []
   258	
   259	    code_file_types = {"python", "bash"}
   260	    _CODE_TOKEN_RE = re.compile(r"[=\(\)\[\]\{\};]|import |def |class |if |for |while |return |print\(")
   261	
   262	    for sf in skill.files:
   263	        if sf.file_type not in code_file_types:
   264	            continue
   265	
   266	        content = sf.read_content()
   267	        if not content:
   268	            continue
   269	
   270	        dangerous_lines: list[tuple[int, str, list[dict]]] = []
   271	
   272	        for line_num, line in enumerate(content.split("\n"), 1):
   273	            stripped = line.strip()
   274	            if not stripped or stripped.startswith("#") or stripped.startswith("//"):
   275	                continue
   276	            if stripped.isascii():
   277	                continue
   278	            if not _CODE_TOKEN_RE.search(stripped):
   279	                continue
   280	
   281	            result = confusables.is_dangerous(stripped, preferred_aliases=["LATIN"])
   282	            if result:
   283	                dangerous_lines.append((line_num, stripped, result))

   285	        min_dangerous_lines = policy.analysis_thresholds.min_dangerous_lines
   286	        if len(dangerous_lines) < min_dangerous_lines:
   287	            continue
   288	
   289	        reported = dangerous_lines[:5]
   290	        line_details = "\n".join(f"  - Line {ln}: {text[:80]}" for ln, text, _ in reported)
   291	        extra = ""
   292	        if len(dangerous_lines) > 5:
   293	            extra = f"\n  ... and {len(dangerous_lines) - 5} more lines"
   294	
   295	        findings.append(
   296	            Finding(
   297	                id=generate_finding_id("HOMOGLYPH_ATTACK", sf.relative_path),
   298	                rule_id="HOMOGLYPH_ATTACK",
   299	                category=ThreatCategory.OBFUSCATION,
   300	                severity=Severity.HIGH,
   301	                title="Unicode homoglyph characters detected in code",
   302	                description=(
   303	                    f"File '{sf.relative_path}' contains characters from mixed Unicode "
   304	                    f"scripts that are visually identical to ASCII letters. "
   305	                    f"This technique can bypass pattern-matching security rules.\n"
   306	                    f"{line_details}{extra}"
   307	                ),
   308	                file_path=sf.relative_path,
   309	                line_number=reported[0][0],
   310	                remediation=(
   311	                    "Replace all non-ASCII lookalike characters with their ASCII "
   312	                    "equivalents. All code should use standard Latin characters."
   313	                ),
   314	                analyzer="static",
   315	                metadata={
   316	                    "affected_lines": len(dangerous_lines),
   317	                    "analysis_method": "confusable_homoglyphs",
   318	                },
   319	            )
   320	        )
   321	
   322	    return findings

## Skills

- cursor-subagent: Run Cursor CLI as a subagent for fast code tasks. Access to Composer 1 (very fast Sonnet-level) and GPT-5.2/Codex models with High/Extra-High reasoning. Focus on FAST variants for rapid iteration. (file: /Users/leemoore/.claude/skills/cursor-subagent/SKILL.md)
